summary: This encyclopedia entry outlines the philosophical evolution of Ludwig Wittgenstein, contrasting his early work (the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus) with his later work (the Philosophical Investigations). It details his shift from viewing language as a logical picture of facts to understanding meaning as use within diverse 'language-games', and his consistent belief that philosophy is a therapeutic activity meant to dissolve conceptual confusions rather than build theories.

principles.statement: The meaning of a word is its use in the language.
principles.rationale: Words do not merely represent objects or house a fixed content; their significance comes from how they are employed in various activities, contexts, and language-games.
principles.source_quote: For a large class of cases of the employment of the word 'meaning'—though not for all—this word can be explained in this way: the meaning of a word is its use in the language

principles.statement: Philosophy is an activity of clarification and therapy, not a body of doctrine.
principles.rationale: The goal of philosophy is not to construct theories or deduce explanations, but to clarify thoughts, critique language, and dissolve confusions caused by linguistic illusions.
principles.source_quote: Philosophy just puts everything before us, and neither explains nor deduces anything.—Since everything lies open to view there is nothing to explain

principles.statement: What cannot be said meaningfully must be passed over in silence.
principles.rationale: Language has logical limits; propositions can only meaningfully picture factual states of affairs. Attempts to express metaphysics, ethics, or aesthetics transcend these bounds into nonsense.
principles.source_quote: What we cannot speak about we must pass over in silence.

frameworks.name: Philosophical Therapy
frameworks.when_to_apply: When encountering philosophical perplexity, feeling trapped by a conceptual problem, or feeling tempted to formulate a general metaphysical theory.
steps: Recognize the philosophical problem as a state of linguistic confusion or disorientation (having the form: 'I don't know my way about').
Resist the urge to theorize or generalize; instead, 'look and see' how the problematic words are actually used in ordinary language.
Marshall reminders and examples of various language-games to break the illusion of a single, essential meaning.
Dissolve the problem completely by showing how language misled us, thereby showing 'the fly the way out of the fly-bottle.'
frameworks.source_quote: The work of the philosopher consists in marshalling reminders for a particular purpose

mental_models.name: Language-games
mental_models.description: Viewing language not as a static, abstract system, but as a diverse collection of rule-governed activities interwoven with human actions and forms of life.
mental_models.example: The builders' language-game, where a builder and his assistant use exactly four terms (block, pillar, slab, beam) to coordinate their practical activity.

mental_models.name: Family Resemblance
mental_models.description: A way to understand how concepts apply to multiple things without sharing a single essential feature. Instead of a strict definition, instances are connected by overlapping and criss-crossing similarities.
mental_models.example: Understanding the concept of a 'game' by looking at the network of similarities between board games, card games, and Olympic games, rather than seeking one common denominator.

mental_models.name: The Ladder Metaphor
mental_models.description: Using a structured philosophical system to achieve a correct perspective, only to realize the system itself is nonsensical and must be discarded once the insight is gained.
mental_models.example: Using the propositions of the Tractatus to 'see the world rightly', and then recognizing them as nonsense and throwing the ladder away.

mental_models.name: Words as Tools
mental_models.description: Viewing words like tools in a toolbox, recognizing that their functions are as diverse as the functions of different physical tools, rather than assuming a uniform appearance implies a uniform function.

heuristics: Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.

signature_quotes.text: The world is all that is the case.
signature_quotes.context: The opening proposition of the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus.

signature_quotes.text: What we cannot speak about we must pass over in silence.
signature_quotes.context: The final proposition (7) of the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus.
An injunction in the Philosophical Investigations to observe actual language use rather than theorizing about it.

signature_quotes.text: A philosophical problem has the form: 'I don't know my way about.'
signature_quotes.context: Describing the nature of philosophical perplexity in the Philosophical Investigations.

signature_quotes.text: to show the fly the way out of the fly-bottle
signature_quotes.context: Describing the ultimate aim of his philosophical method.

anti_patterns.description: The Augustinian picture of language: assuming all words name objects and sentences are merely combinations of names.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It reduces language to a mere representative function, ignoring the vast multiplicity of uses (like asking, thanking, play-acting) and misleading philosophers into false metaphysical speculations.

anti_patterns.description: The craving for generality and dogmatism: seeking essential definitions based on sufficient and necessary conditions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It blinds us to the actual, diverse uses of words in practice, imposing artificial boundaries where there is only fluid 'family resemblance'.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in the possibility of a 'private language' referring only to immediate private sensations.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Meaningful language requires the possibility of judging the correctness of its use, which necessitates public standards and justification that everybody understands.
